critic_raw:
1. BERT-BASED NLP MODEL ON INTERACTION LOGS THAT ARE NOT NATURAL LANGUAGE IS A MODALITY MISMATCH. The design applies a 'BERT-based NLP model that synthesizes interaction text data and engineered metrics.' However, the described interaction data consists of 'course access frequency, forum participation, and assignment engagements' — behavioral event logs, not natural language text. BERT is pre-trained on natural language corpora and its contextual representations are calibrated for linguistic token sequences. Applying BERT to behavioral event sequences (e.g., a sequence of click events, access timestamps, and engagement counts) does not leverage BERT's pre-training in any meaningful way. The design needs to specify what 'text data' exists in the interaction logs — if it is forum posts and assignment text, that is natural language and BERT is applicable; if it is event logs, a sequence model trained from scratch or a tabular model is more appropriate.

2. OVERSAMPLING DROPOUT INSTANCES IN TRAINING DATA FOLLOWED BY STRATIFIED RANDOM SPLIT CREATES INCONSISTENT CLASS DISTRIBUTIONS. The design states: 'dropout instances will be oversampled in the training data' and 'a stratified random split is employed to maintain dropout class distribution across training, validation, and test sets.' These two procedures are in tension. Stratified splitting preserves the original class distribution across splits, then oversampling inflates the training dropout proportion beyond what stratification preserved. If oversampling happens after stratified splitting, the training class distribution is modified while the validation and test class distributions remain at the original proportions. Evaluation on the original class distribution will produce metrics that do not reflect the model's calibration on the oversampled training distribution, potentially inflating precision and deflating recall.

3. DROPOUT LABELING FOR MULTI-COURSE STUDENTS IS AMBIGUOUS AND CREATES SYSTEMATIC MISLABELING RISK. A student is labeled as a dropout if they 'did not complete a course.' In an online learning platform spanning 'several academic years,' students may enroll in multiple courses, complete some and not others, or re-enroll. The design does not specify whether the dropout label is student-level (ever dropped out), course-enrollment-level (did not complete this specific course), or session-level. If it is course-enrollment level and the same student appears multiple times (once for each course enrollment), then stratified random splitting at the enrollment level places the same student's completions and dropouts in different splits. The model learns student-specific patterns, not generalizable early dropout signals.

4. BERT FINE-TUNED ON STUDENT INTERACTIONS REQUIRES A SUBSTANTIALLY DIFFERENT FINE-TUNING PROTOCOL THAN THE DESIGN IMPLIES. The design treats BERT fine-tuning as equivalent in effort to logistic regression hyperparameter tuning 'through cross-validation.' BERT fine-tuning for a classification task requires: domain adaptation pre-training (if available), careful learning rate scheduling to avoid catastrophic forgetting, batch size tuning given GPU memory constraints, and early stopping on a classification head. For a dataset spanning 'several academic years' with behavioral logs, the fine-tuning objective must be carefully specified. A BERT model fine-tuned on the classification task without domain pre-adaptation on educational text will not capture educational domain nuances. The equivalence-of-tuning-effort claim is methodologically shallow.

5. STRATIFIED RANDOM SPLIT ACROSS MULTIPLE ACADEMIC YEARS IGNORES COURSE REVISION AND PLATFORM CHANGES THAT ALTER THE DROPOUT SIGNAL DISTRIBUTION. Online learning platforms undergo curriculum changes, interface redesigns, and grading policy changes across academic years. A stratified random split mixing students from 2019 and 2023 in the same training set will expose the model to systematically different behavioral signatures — students in 2023 may have access to features (discussion forums, mobile apps, AI tutors) not available in 2019. The dropout signal itself may be different: a student who accessed the platform 5 times per week in 2019 may have a different dropout risk profile than an identical access pattern in 2023 when competition for attention is higher. The cross-sectional assumption for a multi-year dataset ignores these structural shifts.
all_issues_raised: BERT applied to behavioral event logs rather than natural language text creates a pre-training modality mismatch
Oversampling after stratified splitting creates inconsistency between training class distribution and evaluation set class distribution
Dropout labeling is ambiguous for multi-course students, risking same-student contamination across splits
BERT fine-tuning complexity is not equivalent to logistic regression cross-validation; the design underdescribes the fine-tuning protocol
Stratified random split across multiple academic years ignores course revisions and platform changes that shift the dropout signal distribution
Oversampling before split or without strict train-only enforcement can leak augmented instances from test distribution into training
Stratified random split on multi-year data ignores cohort effects and course revision impacts on student behavior
BERT fine-tuning on interaction text is biased toward engaged students who generate text — highest-risk low-engagement students are underrepresented
Baseline is demographics-only LR — not a meaningful NLP comparison; behavioral frequency features baseline is missing
AUPRC does not capture prediction timing — early intervention value requires measuring at which course stage dropouts are identified
Oversampling is applied to training but the design does not specify natural class distribution is preserved in validation and test, creating asymmetric evaluation under artificial imbalance
Stratified random split destroys temporal dropout patterns and creates student-level leakage for students with interaction logs spanning the data collection period
BERT is architecturally mismatched to the primary feature types (frequency counts, categorical engagement) — the design implicitly requires an unspecified multi-modal architecture
Demographic-only logistic regression baseline is trivially weak relative to behavioral engagement baselines, making any improvement uninformative about the NLP model's marginal contribution
AUPRC integrates over all thresholds including post-course predictions — early identification value requires evaluation at a specific early-course intervention point that is not specified
